denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We have completed our review of the request your provider submitted for adult ADHD coaching together with continued medication-management visits. After review under the applicable Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin, this request is denied as not medically necessary. Determination date: May 14, 2026.

Aetna medical policy requires documented failure of first-line stimulant pharmacotherapy before the requested services are authorized. On the records reviewed, we did not find that the lower-cost first-line options had been tried and shown to be inadequate; step therapy with first-line pharmacotherapy must be completed before additional services are covered.

The requested coaching component is also addressed under your plan's educational and vocational benefit category, which is a non-covered benefit, rather than the behavioral health benefit category. On that basis the coaching component is separately excluded.

The clinical criteria applied in this review are drawn from published ADHD studies in children and adolescents ages 6 to 17, and the submitted documentation did not satisfy the thresholds in that pediatric criteria set.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific clinical criteria and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision. If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, a peer-to-peer review with an Aetna medical director may be arranged by calling the number on your member ID card.

You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative may submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, along with any additional clinical information. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization after the internal appeal.

Sincerely,
Aetna Behavioral Health Utilization Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 34-year-old woman with combined-type ADHD diagnosed in adulthood, with documented occupational impairment including missed deadlines, a formal performance-improvement plan at work, and an adult ADHD self-report scale (ASRS) score in the highly symptomatic range. Contrary to the denial, the lower-cost pharmacologic options were tried and failed: atomoxetine 80 mg daily was taken for 10 weeks in late 2025 with only partial response, and a subsequent methylphenidate immediate-release trial in early 2026 was limited by palpitations and insomnia that forced discontinuation. Her treating psychiatrist recommends continued medication management together with structured ADHD coaching, which in adult ADHD guidelines is an evidence-based, clinician-directed component of treatment for executive-function deficits, not an educational service. The criteria the insurer applied are derived from ADHD efficacy studies in children ages 6 to 17 and do not reflect adult ADHD treatment standards, where combined pharmacologic and behavioral management is recommended. The coaching is prescribed and supervised as part of her behavioral health care, supporting its classification within the behavioral health benefit rather than an educational benefit.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Denial's core premise (first-line pharmacotherapy not tried) is factually false — context documents a 10-week atomoxetine trial and a discontinued methylphenidate trial. Pediatric (ages 6-17) criteria misapplied to a 34-year-old. Educational/vocational exclusion rebuttable because coaching is clinician-prescribed within behavioral health care. Strong, well-documented clinical/factual vectors.
